mnemonics.title: ACID Properties Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: ACID = A.C.I.D. Remember: 'All Changes In Database' - All (Atomic), Consistency (Correct state), Isolation (Independent), Durable (saved permanently)

mnemonics.title: Transaction States Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: States flow: ACTive → PARTially Committed → COMmitted (or Aborted). Think: 'Active People Can Abort' or 'Active Party Causes Abort' for states.

mnemonics.title: Concurrency Problems Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Three main problems: LUI - Lost Update, Uncommitted dependency (dirty read), Inconsistent analysis. Remember: 'Loving Unmarried Individuals' helps recall L-U-I.

acronyms.usage: The four fundamental properties that guarantee reliable database transactions
Language used to manage and manipulate relational databases
Software that manages database operations and ensures ACID properties
Protocol ensuring atomic commitment in distributed database systems
Database system based on relational model with transaction support

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a transaction as a single ball rolling down a path. If it hits any obstacle (error), it bounces back to the start - nothing was moved. If it reaches the end, the ball has moved permanently.

Think of a database table as a building with four pillars: Atomicity (all-or-nothing foundation), Consistency (structural integrity), Isolation (separate rooms), Durability (concrete foundation that never breaks).
Serial = one car on a road (no accidents but slow). Parallel = multiple cars (fast but risk collisions). Concurrency control = traffic rules to prevent accidents.
Imagine dominoes falling - T1 rolls back (first domino), causing T2 to also rollback (second domino falls), and T3 (third domino) - one倒下 causes others.

analogies.analogy: Like a bank transfer: money leaves one account AND arrives in another account - both happen together or neither happens. Like a shopping cart: all items are purchased together or none are.
Like flipping a coin - it's either heads or tails, nothing in between. A transaction is like this: either fully complete or fully cancelled.
Like people using separate stalls in a bathroom - each person (transaction) operates independently without knowing what others are doing.
Like writing in a permanent marker on paper - once written (committed), the ink doesn't fade even if you close the notebook (system shutdown).
Like reading a signed contract - you only act on information from someone who has finalized (committed) their part, not from someone who might change their mind.
